title: Classes & Objects
description: Understanding the building blocks of Object-Oriented Programming

concepts.title: What is a Class?
concepts.explanation: A class is a blueprint or template for creating objects. It defines properties (attributes) and behaviors (methods) that objects of that class will have.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: Think of a class like an architectural blueprint for a house. The blueprint defines what the house will look like, how many rooms it has, etc. But the blueprint itself is not a house - it's a template for building houses.
keyPoints: A class defines the structure and behavior of objects
Classes encapsulate data and functions together
A class is a user-defined data type
Classes provide a way to organize and structure code

concepts.title: What is an Object?
concepts.explanation: An object is an instance of a class. When you create an object from a class, you're creating a specific entity with the properties and behaviors defined in that class.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: If a class is a blueprint, then objects are the actual houses built from that blueprint. Each house (object) has its own specific address, color, and residents, but they all follow the same basic design.
keyPoints: Objects are instances of classes
Each object has its own copy of instance variables
Objects can have different values for the same properties
Objects interact with each other through methods

concepts.title: Class Members
concepts.explanation: Class members include attributes (data members/fields) and methods (member functions). Attributes store data, while methods define behaviors.
keyPoints: Attributes/Fields: Variables that hold object data
Methods: Functions that define object behavior
Members can be instance-level or class-level (static)

cppCode.title: Class Definition in C++
cppCode.explanation: In C++, classes are defined using the 'class' keyword. Members are grouped by access specifiers (public, private, protected). The class definition ends with a semicolon.

javaCode.title: Class Definition in Java
javaCode.explanation: In Java, each public class must be in a file with the same name. Objects are created using the 'new' keyword. Java has no semicolon after class definition.

comparison.cpp:
ClassName obj; // Stack allocation
ClassName* ptr = new ClassName(); // Heap allocation
comparison.java: ClassName obj = new ClassName(); // Always heap allocation

comparison.cpp: Manual (delete for heap objects) or automatic (stack)

practiceQuestions.title: Create a Student Class
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a class called 'Student' with the following:
- Attributes: name (string), age (int), grade (float/double)
- Method: displayDetails() that prints all the student information
- Create 2 student objects and display their details
hints: Start by defining the class with the attributes
Create a method that prints each attribute
In main, create objects and set their values

practiceQuestions.title: Create a Rectangle Class
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a class called 'Rectangle' with:
- Attributes: length and width (both doubles)
- Methods: 
  - calculateArea() - returns the area
  - calculatePerimeter() - returns the perimeter
  - display() - prints length, width, area, and perimeter
hints: Area = length × width
Perimeter = 2 × (length + width)
Methods can call other methods

intermediate.title: Intermediate Concepts
intermediate.description: Access modifiers, constructors, and this keyword

concepts.title: Access Modifiers in Classes
concepts.explanation: Access modifiers control the visibility and accessibility of class members. They are fundamental to encapsulation.
keyPoints: public: Accessible from anywhere
private: Accessible only within the class
protected: Accessible within class and derived classes
C++ default: private | Java default: package-private

concepts.title: The 'this' Keyword
concepts.explanation: The 'this' keyword refers to the current object instance. It's used to differentiate between instance variables and parameters with the same name.
keyPoints: 'this' is a pointer in C++ (this->member)
'this' is a reference in Java (this.member)
Used to resolve naming conflicts
Can be used to pass current object to other methods

concepts.title: Getters and Setters
concepts.explanation: Getter and setter methods provide controlled access to private data members. This is a key aspect of encapsulation.
keyPoints: Getters return the value of private members
Setters modify the value with validation
Allows control over how data is accessed/modified
Essential for data hiding and encapsulation

cppCode.title: Encapsulation with Getters/Setters in C++
cppCode.explanation: In C++, 'this' is a pointer, so we use 'this->' to access members. Private members can only be accessed through public methods.

javaCode.title: Encapsulation with Getters/Setters in Java
javaCode.explanation: In Java, 'this' is a reference, so we use 'this.' to access members. Java also has package-private access (no modifier) which is different from C++.

practiceQuestions.title: Create an Employee Class with Encapsulation
practiceQuestions.description:
Create an Employee class with:
- Private attributes: id, name, salary, department
- Constructor that initializes all fields
- Getters for all fields
- Setters with validation:
  - name: cannot be empty
  - salary: cannot be negative, max 1,000,000
  - department: must be one of 'IT', 'HR', 'Finance', 'Marketing'
- Method: giveRaise(percentage) - increases salary by given percentage
hints: Use 'this' keyword to differentiate parameters from fields
Validation should happen in setters
giveRaise should check if percentage is valid (0-100)

practiceQuestions.title: Library Book Management
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a Book class with proper encapsulation:
- Private: isbn, title, author, isAvailable, borrowerName
- Methods:
  - borrowBook(borrowerName): marks book as borrowed if available
  - returnBook(): marks book as available, clears borrower
  - getDetails(): returns formatted string with all details
- Validation: ISBN must be 13 characters, title/author non-empty
hints: isAvailable should be a boolean
borrowBook should fail if already borrowed
returnBook should fail if not borrowed

advanced.title: Advanced Concepts
advanced.description: Static members, object relationships, and memory

concepts.title: Static Members
concepts.explanation: Static members belong to the class itself, not to any specific object. They are shared among all instances of the class.
keyPoints: Static variables: shared by all objects of the class
Static methods: can be called without creating an object
Static methods cannot access non-static members directly
Used for counters, utility methods, constants

concepts.title: Object Relationships
concepts.explanation: Classes can have relationships with other classes: Association, Aggregation, and Composition.
keyPoints: Association: 'uses-a' relationship (weak coupling)
Aggregation: 'has-a' relationship (can exist independently)
Composition: 'owns-a' relationship (strong ownership, lifecycle dependency)

concepts.title: Memory Management Differences
concepts.explanation: C++ and Java handle memory very differently. Understanding this is crucial for writing efficient code.
keyPoints: C++: Manual memory management with new/delete
Java: Automatic garbage collection
C++: Stack vs Heap allocation choice
Java: All objects on heap, primitives on stack

cppCode.title: Advanced Class Concepts in C++
cppCode.explanation: This demonstrates static members, composition (Car owns Engine - deletes it in destructor), and aggregation (Department has Employees but doesn't own them).

javaCode.title: Advanced Class Concepts in Java
javaCode.explanation: Java handles memory automatically with garbage collection. Composition is shown with 'final' keyword ensuring Engine can't be reassigned. Aggregation uses ArrayList.

comparison.cpp: Must initialize outside class: int Class::var = 0;
comparison.java: Can initialize inline: private static int var = 0;

comparison.cpp: Manual with delete, destructors called immediately

practiceQuestions.title: Implement a Singleton Logger
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a Logger class using the Singleton pattern:
- Only one instance should exist
- Methods: log(message), error(message), warn(message)
- Track total log count (static)
- Thread-safe in Java (bonus)
- Include timestamp with each log message
hints: Private constructor prevents external instantiation
Static method returns the single instance
Use static variable to hold the instance
Java: consider synchronized or enum-based singleton

practiceQuestions.title: University Management System
practiceQuestions.description:
Design classes showing different relationships:

1. University (Composition with Departments)
   - Has multiple departments
   - When university is destroyed, departments are destroyed

2. Department (Aggregation with Professors)
   - Has multiple professors
   - Professors can exist without department

3. Professor (Association with Courses)
   - Teaches multiple courses
   - Can switch courses

4. Include static member to track total universities

Implement with proper encapsulation and demonstrate all relationships.
hints: Composition: Container creates and destroys components
Aggregation: Container holds references but doesn't own
Association: Objects reference each other loosely
Use vectors/lists to hold multiple objects

keyDifferences.title: Object Creation
keyDifferences.cpp: Stack: Car c; | Heap: Car* c = new Car();
keyDifferences.java: Always heap: Car c = new Car();

keyDifferences.title: Default Access
keyDifferences.cpp: private for class, public for struct

bestPractices: Always use meaningful names for classes, attributes, and methods
Follow encapsulation - make data private, provide controlled access
Keep classes focused - Single Responsibility Principle
Prefer composition over inheritance when possible

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting semicolon after C++ class definition
Trying to access private members directly
Modifying static members thinking they're per-instance
commonMistakes.correct: Static members are shared across all instances
